title: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) - Read Any User's Draft
description: Any authenticated user can read drafts belonging to other users by manipulating the numeric draft ID in the /edit_draft/<id> endpoint. The application does not verify that the requesting user owns the draft being accessed. An attacker can enumerate all draft IDs (starting from 1) and read the title and content of every draft in the system, including drafts belonging to administrator accounts.
steps: 1. Register and log in as a regular user (e.g., testuser). 2. Create a draft to discover the sequential draft ID (e.g., ID=2). 3. Decrement the ID to access other users' drafts: GET /edit_draft/1. 4. The response displays the admin user's draft content (title: 'admin_draft') without any authorization check. Verified with: curl -s -c cookies.txt -b cookies.txt http://blog:5003/edit_draft/1

title: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) - Modify Any User's Draft
description: Any authenticated user can modify drafts belonging to other users by sending a POST request to /edit_draft/<id> with any draft ID. The application performs no ownership check before updating the draft. This allows a low-privileged attacker to tamper with content in any user's drafts, including the administrator's drafts, causing unauthorized data modification.
steps: 1. Register and log in as a regular user (e.g., testuser). 2. Send a POST request to /edit_draft/1 (admin's draft): curl -s -c cookies.txt -b cookies.txt -X POST http://blog:5003/edit_draft/1 --data-urlencode 'title=Hacked' --data-urlencode 'content=Tampered'. 3. Verify admin's draft is modified by visiting /edit_draft/1 — the title and content have been overwritten. Draft 1 (admin_draft) title was successfully changed to 'Hacked by testuser'.

title: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) - Access Any User's Draft List
description: Any authenticated user can view the complete list of drafts for any other user by navigating to /<username>/drafts. The application does not restrict access to a user's draft index page based on the logged-in session. An attacker can enumerate known or guessed usernames to enumerate all their draft titles and associated draft IDs, facilitating further IDOR attacks on individual drafts.
steps: 1. Register and log in as testuser. 2. Navigate to http://blog:5003/admin/drafts. 3. The page displays 'admin's Drafts' with all drafts listed including draft ID links. 4. Also accessible: /user/drafts. This works because there is no authorization check on the /<username>/drafts route.

title: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) - No Token Protection on State-Changing Operations
description: All state-changing forms in the application (create draft, edit draft) lack CSRF tokens. The session cookie has no SameSite attribute, making it vulnerable to cross-site request forgery. An attacker can trick a logged-in user into performing unintended actions (creating, modifying drafts) by hosting a malicious page that auto-submits a form to the target application. Combined with the IDOR write vulnerability, this can be used to modify any user's drafts.
steps: 1. Log in as testuser and capture the session cookie. 2. From a different origin (evil.com), submit a POST to http://blog:5003/create_draft with title and content parameters, sending only the victim's session cookie (no CSRF token): curl -s -X POST http://blog:5003/create_draft -H 'Origin: http://evil.attacker.com' -H 'Referer: http://evil.attacker.com/csrf.html' -b cookies.txt --data-urlencode 'title=CSRF PoC' --data-urlencode 'content=Created via CSRF'. 3. The draft is created successfully. The server returns HTTP 302 and the draft appears in the user's list.

title: No Brute Force Protection on Login Endpoint
description: The login endpoint (/login) has no rate limiting, account lockout mechanism, or CAPTCHA. An attacker can make unlimited rapid login attempts against any account without triggering any defensive response. This allows automated credential stuffing and brute force attacks against all user accounts. 50 consecutive failed login attempts were made with no HTTP 429 or lockout response.
steps: 1. Send repeated POST requests to /login with the target username and varying passwords: for i in $(seq 1 50); do curl -s -X POST http://blog:5003/login --data-urlencode 'username=admin' --data-urlencode "password=attempt$i" -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n'; done. 2. All 50 requests return HTTP 200 (login page shown again — wrong password), with no rate limiting or lockout. 3. No HTTP 429 Too Many Requests was ever returned.

title: Username Enumeration via Registration Error Message
description: The registration endpoint returns a distinct error message 'Username already exists. Please choose a different one.' when a username is already taken, allowing an attacker to enumerate valid usernames. By attempting to register usernames one at a time, an attacker can build a list of all valid accounts in the system to use in targeted attacks.
steps: 1. Send a POST request to /register with an existing username: curl -s -X POST http://blog:5003/register --data-urlencode 'username=admin' --data-urlencode 'password=Test1234!' --data-urlencode 'confirm_password=Test1234!'. 2. The response contains: 'Username already exists. Please choose a different one.' 3. For non-existing usernames, the registration succeeds (HTTP 302). 4. Confirmed existing accounts: admin, user, testuser.

title: Username Enumeration via Login Timing Side-Channel
description: The login endpoint reveals whether a username exists based on response time. When a valid username is submitted, the server performs a bcrypt password hash comparison (taking ~55-60ms). For non-existent usernames, the server returns immediately (~4ms) without performing any hash comparison. This timing difference allows an attacker to reliably enumerate valid usernames without needing to see any error message.
steps: 1. Measure response time for an existing username: time curl -s -X POST http://blog:5003/login --data-urlencode 'username=admin' --data-urlencode 'password=wrong' -o /dev/null. Result: ~58ms. 2. Measure response time for a non-existent username: time curl -s -X POST http://blog:5003/login --data-urlencode 'username=nonexistentuser9999' --data-urlencode 'password=wrong' -o /dev/null. Result: ~4ms. 3. The ~54ms difference reliably distinguishes valid from invalid usernames.

title: No Password Complexity Requirements (Weak Password Policy)
description: The application accepts passwords of any length and complexity, including single-character passwords. There are no minimum length requirements enforced server-side (beyond what is displayed in the HTML). This allows users to set trivially guessable passwords that significantly lower the barrier for brute force attacks. A test account was registered with the password 'a' (single character) successfully.
steps: 1. Register a new user with a single-character password: curl -s -X POST http://blog:5003/register --data-urlencode 'username=weakpass1' --data-urlencode 'password=a' --data-urlencode 'confirm_password=a'. 2. The server returns HTTP 302 (registration successful). 3. Login with the weak password: curl -s -X POST http://blog:5003/login --data-urlencode 'username=weakpass1' --data-urlencode 'password=a' -D -. 4. Login succeeds and session is established.

title: Session Cookie Missing 'Secure' and 'SameSite' Attributes
description: The application's session cookie is issued without the 'Secure' flag and without a 'SameSite' attribute. The absence of the Secure flag means the cookie can be transmitted over unencrypted HTTP connections, exposing it to network interception (MITM attacks). The absence of SameSite attribute (or SameSite=None) means the cookie is sent in all cross-origin requests, enabling CSRF attacks. The cookie is: session=...; HttpOnly; Path=/ — no Secure, no SameSite.
steps: 1. Log in and capture the Set-Cookie header: curl -s -X POST http://blog:5003/login --data-urlencode 'username=testuser' --data-urlencode 'password=Test1234!' -D -. 2. Observe the Set-Cookie header: 'session=<token>; HttpOnly; Path=/'. 3. Note the absence of 'Secure' (allows HTTP transmission) and 'SameSite' (enables CSRF). The SameSite omission is also what makes the CSRF attack possible.

title: Missing Security Headers (X-Frame-Options, CSP, X-Content-Type-Options)
description: The application does not set any security-related HTTP response headers. Missing headers include: X-Frame-Options (enables clickjacking attacks where the app can be embedded in an iframe), Content-Security-Policy (no restriction on script sources, allows XSS exploitation), X-Content-Type-Options (allows MIME-type sniffing), and Strict-Transport-Security (no HTTPS enforcement). All pages are served with only Content-Type, Content-Length, Vary, and Connection headers.
steps: 1. Inspect response headers: curl -s -D - http://blog:5003/ -o /dev/null. 2. Response headers show only: Server, Date, Content-Type, Content-Length, Vary, Connection. 3. No X-Frame-Options header — application can be embedded in an iframe for clickjacking. 4. No Content-Security-Policy header — no restriction on inline scripts or external resource loading. 5. No X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff — browsers may perform MIME sniffing.

title: Server Version Disclosure via HTTP Response Header
description: The application discloses the full server technology stack in the HTTP 'Server' response header on every request. The header reveals: 'Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17', exposing both the WSGI framework version and the Python interpreter version. This information helps attackers identify known vulnerabilities specific to these versions and target their attacks accordingly. Python 3.8.17 is also an older, potentially unmaintained version.
steps: 1. Send any HTTP request to the server: curl -s -I http://blog:5003/. 2. Observe the response header: 'Server: Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17'. 3. This reveals the exact versions of Werkzeug and Python in use, enabling version-specific vulnerability research.
